sentences.text: Not to Russia.
Towards-- there we ran away from the Germans.
Because by the already, which it could have been already 1938, we knew already they have concentration camps for the German Jews.
And maybe there wasn't any camps, like they were later, to-- you know, like--
Exterminating them.
But they did have concentration camps already-- whoever did not have a chance to go to another country.
So he just warned us we should not stay in Lodz.
We should run away, towards west, was it, I guess.
And not too many people did, though.
Because they had families.
So you can't just leave the family and go away, unless it was like it was later.
The people saw already there's no any-- there's no way that they're going to survive if they stay together.
So the parents encouraged the children just to go.
Yes, we knew there was going to be a war.
In Lodz.
It was on a Friday.
On a September Friday, it was just before the holidays.
Because-- it was September the 1st?
Because when I came home, it was just for Rosh Hashanah.
And it took me a few days to get home.
And-- and I did not leave right away.
I left, the second day, although the-- with encouragement of that family that I stayed with.
And they kept on saying, you go, you go.
Your family is there.
So that's why I left.
First, I went by bus, [INAUDIBLE] buses.
So I went by bus to Warsaw.
And when I came to Warsaw-- I mean, there were-- the buses were packed.
I came to Warsaw.
They bombard already-- bombed Warsaw, the Germans.
So we were laying on the-- where the bus-- the bus station.
We were laying there about a half a day.
Because we couldn't get up.
We were laying on the ground between everything.
And when that stopped, for whatever time it stopped, I thought of an address that I remembered, that a friend of ours lived with those people from our city, from Dubienka.
They really lived near Dubienka's [NON-ENGLISH].
They were really very rich people-- big farms.
They owned big farms.
But a lot of them did not want to stay on the farms.
And they left to Warsaw.
And they lived in Warsaw.
And I remembered the address by heart, because I know where they were writing to their son all the time.
So I went in there.
And I don't know how they let everybody know, people that were from Dubienka, young-- you know, young adults that were working in Warsaw.
And I really don't know.
Maybe they already had telephones over there.
We didn't have any.
So-- and I'm sure that the people that worked from our town in Warsaw didn't have a telephone.
Maybe they had sent somebody-- messengers or whatever.
They right away let everybody know that I'm there.
And a lot of my friends that I still have them-- they were in Warsaw.
There they put me on the train, through a window-- pushed me in through a window.
The train was packed with people.
And they were standing there at the train, by the door.
Polish people-- they only thought that it's only for military that was called to the service.
Because if one-- let's say like my husband was in the service, and he was called back during a war, during an emergency, let's say if he was in Dubienka, they would call him to Lodz.
The would-- he would have to come to Lodz to join his--
--company, his unit.
Or maybe the people from Lodz had to go the place where he was at.
So there was actually a lot of people-- the military-- they were called to, on the train also.
But they threw me in through a window.
And I got there before-- on that train, before Lublin.
And some place around Lublin-- I don't remember exactly the name-- there was an air force.
And they had a lot of airplanes.
There were a lot-- I don't know how many airplanes Poland had.
But there was-- there were airplanes.
So they bombed there-- they were bombing the place over there.
So we had to leave the train and went into the woods until it stopped, the bombardment, and airplane-- German airplanes flew away.
We got back into the train.
And I got into one car-- from the train, I saw my package.
Because I didn't know I even have it.
Because they-- after they threw me-- me in, they pushed all that, whatever I had, my belongings, into the train.
And this train took me to a place.
It was 16 kilometers from Dubienka.
So-- and from there, we had to take already a horse and buggy that a-- were people like cabs that used to go from there, from the train to our town.
And I got home with them.
Then it was Yom Tov.
And I was happy to be home.
And who knew that in Dubienka will be a war also.
I mean, you know, we just thought, maybe Lodz-- big cities.
And it's close to Germany.
But in a few days, we heard that the Russians are coming to Dubienka, that the Germans and the Russians split Poland.
They made a pact that this will belong to Russia, this half.
And the other, whatever, belongs to Germans.
And the Russians are going to stay there.
There was a-- well, Dubienka actually was in one side of the Bug.
And [NON-ENGLISH] was on the other side of the Bug.
Bug is a big river-- not that big, but according to Dubienka, there were a little rivers.
So this was a big river.
And the Bug was actually the border, where they made the division of Poland.
But we didn't know where the division is going to be.
So since the Russians came to Dubienka also, we thought they going to stay there.
But it didn't take long.
And they had to go back.
So when they had to go back, and we knew that the Germans are coming in to Dubienka, they-- I, for first, didn't want to stay there.
Because I knew a lot about-- you know, I-- so I didn't want to stay there.
And then my sisters-- she wasn't married yet, also, the middle sister-- she, too, didn't want to stay there.
So we both left.
We left--
My parents still stayed there.
They figured that they could always leave.
And then they had my sister-- older sister had already two children.
So they stayed with them.
We lived-- we lived together.
So we left.
Everything was so fast.
Here it was maybe one week.
And my parents, and my sister, and my brother-in-law took the children and decided to leave also.
Because my father was a very-- very well read.
And he knew everything what's-- what is going on.
But it's hard to leave a home and just go, not knowing where.
So-- but when my older sister decided that they want to leave-- her husband, the children-- so they decided they'll go with them.
They don't want to stay behind alone.
So they went.
They just crossed the Bug and went in a village, right after they crossed the river.
They rented a room.
And that's where they stayed.
I really don't know.
Maybe [?
Krynitza, ?] and maybe [?
Pestrok. ?] I really don't know.
I think it was more-- [?
Krynitza, ?] I think it was called.
Because I was never there.
Me?
I and my sister went to Lubon.
Lubon, we had an aunt and uncle.
And so we figured we have a place where to go.
So we stayed in Lubon.
And then they all kind of things came up to-- you should register to go to Russia.
And I didn't want to.
And my sister didn't either.
But my sister-- as soon as my parents and my older sister would cross the Bug, as long as they weren't out by the Germans, my middle sister went to there.
And I went to Wlodzimierz.
Wlodzimierz, I had a-- a woman that I used to learn-- she used to teach me how to embroider and how to make fancy lingerie.
Yeah, we used to make beautiful stuff.
After school, I used to go in there.
And then, later, I used to try to do it a little longer.
And I learned really a lot from her.
So she got married to Wlodzimierz, her husband from Wlodzimierz.
So I went there.
And she asked me maybe if I wanted to stay with her.
And she had twins.
And she couldn't really do work-- a lot of work.
So she asked me, maybe I would like to stay with her and work.
So while I will be working, she will help me.
And we'll do together.
So I said, fine.
Because I wanted to be in the city.
I didn't want to go back to a village someplace where my parents were.
So I-- I stayed with her-- What was her name?
And worked.
The--
The woman was [?
Ruthie ?] Kramer-- Kramer.
I really don't remember.
My husband would remember her husband's name, her married name.
But her maiden name was Kramer.
And I worked there already-- I-- see, over there, the Russians were already.
And they were-- they didn't bother you to go to a-- to a-- what are they called?
[NON-ENGLISH], or some place where they had whatever work to do.
So I worked there with her for a while.
Then a lot of people used to-- came in from [?
Hrubieszow. ?] The Germans did take some people-- I'd say a few hundred people from a city.
And they would make him run as long as they could.
Whoever couldn't, they were killing on the road.
My husband's uncle came at that time.
He wanted to-- they took him to run to-- it was he survived.
He survived.
And he came to that town, to the city.
And so we knew what was going on over there.
So meanwhile, I was secure.
And then-- and the--
it happened-- I don't know really how long it took-- but it happened that my parents could not longer stay in that place where they were.
Because the Russians were preparing probably for some kind of-- whether defensive or offensive.
They knew already what was going on-- what going to be a war, or something.
[INAUDIBLE] this was already in 1940.
So they evacuated all the people that were there, the refugees.
And they sent them to Russia.
So when my parents came to Wlodzimierz, where the train-- so I came over to the train.
And I wanted to go with them.
But at that time, there were two things-- my-- my husband, [INAUDIBLE] Gordon, was in jail.
They-- a boyfriend of his crossed the border.
And they caught him-- the Russians caught him by crossing.
And they put him in jail.
In their jail-- they didn't have any jails over there.
So they took a school.
And they kept all the people they'd arrested on the border.
So when my husband went over there, they were in the yard outside.
And he saw his boyfriend over there.
So he went home and brought a bread and went over to give it to him.
So he gave him the bread already.
But the guard master saw him.
And he was following him, ran after him, and arrested him.
They said, he's a spy.
The other one is a spy that's in jail.
And he was supporting this guy-- gave him maybe something, whatever.
So they arrested him.
So he was sitting in jail.
It wasn't a jail, too.
It was in a basement.
And so he was laying in a basement there.
So when I came to the train, and I was talking to the family.
And I ?] want to go with them.
So my father says, well, [?
Godol ?] is in jail.
And you don't want to leave without him, or you want to whatever, because we were going already.
And they knew it.
So he said, you could always-- a child could always come to parents.
If it's going to be good, you could always come to us.
And if it's not good, what would you want to be there?
So I listened to him.
And I stayed.
So meanwhile, my husband-- they got somebody, a big man over there, his cousins, because he had cousins that lived there.
And they lived at a cousin's house.
So they found somebody to release them from jail.
So he got out of jail.
And-- and I was working still.
And I-- as he was working, too, some place, for the Russians.
So-- but meanwhile, my parents came in Russia, to a very bad place.
They took them to Vologda, in the woods.
There they had to go 10 kilometers to buy, let's say, a loaf of bread and 10 pounds of potatoes.
And they couldn't buy 20 pounds.
They couldn't buy-- and it was winter.
And the snow-- was a lot of snow over there.
Because they are bad winters.
And so they really were in bad shape.
So I used to be able to send them food, send them packages.
Because here, there was enough food, in Ukraine.
So I used to send them packages there.
So I heard from people that are leaving these places-- they running away.
And my father wrote me that he does not believe in it.
Because once you run away, you already have no place, really, where to go-- that he's going to stay there the year that they promised.
That after a year, they could move any place they want-- big cities, small-- any place.
But they have to stay there a year and work.
I don't know what they did.
They probably were cutting woods over there, trees to make lumber or something.
So they did.
They stayed a year over there.
And then they moved to a big city, Vologda.
And they were working.
They were pretty happy there.
But my father remembered-- because when he was young, he was in the Russian Army, years ago.
And they were in [?
Crimea, ?] by the Black Sea.
And he remembered it was-- there it was very good.
Everything was-- he just-- he figured it's probably the same.
Maybe it wasn't, but he just took a chance.
He decided he wants to go there, since he wasn't restricted already.
So he went there.
And he found out that he wants to live there.
So my father, and mother, and my brother went to [INAUDIBLE].
And my sister and her husband and their two children stayed in Vologda until they will get adjusted.
And then they will come to them.
And I used to-- had letters for them just before the war that it was beautiful, just beautiful.
First of all, it was a warm country there.
And over there, it was so bitter cold.
And the second of all, there was a lot of work, and a lot of food, w which in Russia really was not equally distributed.
Whatever they had, they didn't transport it to another part of the country that easily.
And then the war broke out.
In 1941, the war broke out.
And I did plan to go.
Because I was going to school.
Later on, I stopped working.
And I started going to school.
And I went to school.
So when our-- in this last year in school, I decided I will go for vacation to my parents.
And I used to get letters from my husband also, from Siberia or wherever they were.
And they had a teacher that was from Siberia, from Novosibirsk.
And they used to tell me, it's beautiful over there.
I mean, Novosibirsk is a big city.
But they were not in the cities.
They were on the Tigris someplace.
So-- but he said that you're allowed to go there.
I mean, if you have a Russian passport and you have everything, you're allowed to go.
So-- well, I just didn't able to tell you the part how my husband got to Siberia.
But that'll probably be later.
He'll tell you later.
So-- and that's-- that's the way I never saw my parents again, or my-- my family, my sisters.
In the whole family, my sisters and brother survived in Russia.
Because my older sister-- my parents died in Russia, in Siberia, after the war started in 1941.
Well, because, see, [INAUDIBLE] was bombed.
It's the same-- the same day that it was bombed that our city was bombed in Kovel, where I was.
They made like a blitzkrieg.
They did it right in the same night.
They bombed the Russian cities and they bombed the Ukrainian cities, to make kind of like a-- mix the people up.
It was in June-- the 20th or the 21st.
When it was just the night.
The school was out today.
And tomorrow, they bombed the city already.
Well, I-- I worked in Wlodzimierz.
I went after, I guess, the first winter or the first-- after my parents were already in there.
A whole winter, I worked there.
And in the summer, I decided I want to go to school.
So I didn't want to go in Wlodzimierz.
Because now, Wlodzimierz, I didn't have anybody already, like from my family.
I was just with Ruthie Kramer.
And my-- my husband's family, they were sent already to Siberia.
And how they were sent-- also, they were sent by force.
Because at that time, the Russians already did not trust the refugees.
Because when they told them to take out Russian passports, they didn't want to take out passports.
The Jews still didn't believe that this is already finished with their home.
They figured well, if they stay in Wlodzimierz, they could always go back home when the war is finished.
I mean, it's-- it was just-- I mean, I don't know the feeling.
Because I will never live through a war.
But all the people what-- they lived already World War I, like my parents, or my husband's parents, or other parents-- they knew that the war is finished, you could go back home.
But that was a different kind of war.
When do you mean?
And still, when the Russians were?
Yeah, at this time--
When they--
To Lodz.
Now, in this time, when my husband-- they took my husband.
Did he tell you that he was almost killed?
He jump out of the window when they took-- wanted to take him to Russia and he didn't want to go.
He didn't want to go.
So while the-- the people were there to take him out of the house, he jumped out of the window.
And they shot-- they tried to shoot him.
But he fell-- probably, he was a previous soldier, so he must have fell on the ground.
And they didn't hit him.
So when I came, the same thing-- when they took him and I didn't know.
Because what did we have, telephone?
We didn't.
Unless he came over to talk to me, or I came over there, we didn't know anything from each other.
So when they were already in the-- in the cars, the train cars, and I found out that they took them already there, too.
So I went over.
And I found [INAUDIBLE]-- I went over with a passport.
Because they came every-- they came in every house to look for refugees.
So I took a passport from a girl that I lived with, a niece from Ruthie Kramer.
I took it with me.
And I went to the train.
I went to the train.
And I said that I want to go through.
I mean, I just didn't know what to do.
So everybody was looking at me like crazy.
If they don't bother you, why would you want to but yourself go?
And nobody even asked me for a passport.
I don't know why, but nobody asked me for a passport.
So I had my-- have a passport in my pocket, but nobody asked me.
The only thing they did is chase me away from the refugees.
They said, you go away from here.
You go away from here.
You don't belong here.
So I went away.
And what I knew-- I don't know how I let my uncle in Lubon.
But I told you I had an aunt and uncle in Lubon, where we came first there.
He probably knew by himself that they probably did, from Lubon, the same thing with the refugees.
So he came with the horse and buggy.
And he was a cripple from World War I, where they was-- he was-- he was walking on crutches.
He had to come all night, sitting on that horse and buggy all night, to bring me my sister's passport.
Because my sister-- middle sister used to go there to visit, for Pesach, for [?
Sukkot. ?] When I was in Lodz, she used to go there.
And when you came in, in Poland, in a city, from another city-- young-- young people-- you had to register at a police station.
You always had to register.
Because they just didn't trust people, whatever-- they are communists, or different things, or whatever.
So she was registered over there, let's say before the war, in 1936 or 1937.
So he went to the city hall, and made a passport on her name, and brought it to me to Wlodzimierz.
So I already had a passport that they couldn't take me any place there.
So I had this passport.
So and then after my parents already didn't need my help, I went to Kovel from Wlodzimierz.
I had a passport.
I had an uncle over there-- to-- I mean, Kovel.
I had an uncle there.
A different uncle?
A different, yeah.
This was in Lubon.
This was-- in Lubon it was my father's sister.
And in Kovel, it was my mother's brother.
And their name was Spiegel.
And so I went there.
And they had a son, Ben-Zion.
And so when I came there, he really suggested-- because he started going to school.
He was much older than I.
But he also wanted to learn Russian.
So I came there.
And I stayed there, with my cousin's in-laws.
Because they had a big house.
And it was close to school.
So I stayed with them.
And I started going to school over there, in Kovel.
And that's the way what I got to be in Kovel.
Well, it was 1940.
It was in 1940 when I got to Kovel.
I stayed there until-- in '41, was the war broke out, in June.
And probably about a half a year, I worked for the Germans.
